Atelectasis.definition: Partial or complete collapse of a lung or lung lobe, resulting in reduced gas exchange.

severity_grades.mild: Subsegmental or linear atelectasis, often incidental
severity_grades.moderate: Lobar atelectasis with visible volume loss
severity_grades.severe: Complete lung collapse with mediastinal shift

follow_up: Repeat CXR in 4-6 weeks if incidental finding
CT chest if persistent or suspicious for endobronchial lesion
Incentive spirometry and chest physiotherapy for post-operative cases

interactions.Effusion: May be compressive atelectasis secondary to effusion — treat the effusion first
interactions.Mass: Consider post-obstructive atelectasis — CT with contrast recommended

Cardiomegaly.definition: Enlargement of the cardiac silhouette, cardiothoracic ratio > 0.5 on PA view.
follow_up: Echocardiography to assess ventricular function
BNP/NT-proBNP levels
Cardiology referral if new finding
Serial CXR to monitor progression

interactions.Effusion: Cardiomegaly + bilateral effusions strongly suggests congestive heart failure
interactions.Edema: Cardiomegaly + pulmonary edema = decompensated heart failure — emergent management

Consolidation.definition: Opacification of airspaces with air bronchograms, indicating alveolar filling with fluid, pus, or cells.
follow_up: Clinical correlation — infection vs aspiration vs hemorrhage
Blood cultures and sputum if infectious etiology suspected
CT chest if non-resolving after appropriate treatment
Follow-up CXR in 6-8 weeks to confirm resolution

interactions.Pneumonia: Consolidation is the radiographic hallmark of pneumonia — treat as pneumonia if clinical picture fits
interactions.Effusion: Parapneumonic effusion — consider thoracentesis if large

Edema.definition: Pulmonary edema — fluid accumulation in the lung parenchyma, typically from cardiogenic or non-cardiogenic causes.

severity_grades.moderate: Bilateral perihilar haziness, Kerley B lines

follow_up: Urgent cardiology assessment
Diuretics (furosemide) if cardiogenic
Echocardiography
Repeat CXR after treatment to assess response
ICU admission if severe respiratory distress

interactions.Cardiomegaly: Edema + cardiomegaly = cardiogenic pulmonary edema — classic heart failure presentation

Effusion.definition: Pleural effusion — fluid collection in the pleural space, seen as blunting of costophrenic angles or layering opacity.

severity_grades.mild: Blunting of costophrenic angle (< 500 mL estimated)

follow_up: Lateral decubitus film or ultrasound to confirm free-flowing
Thoracentesis if moderate-to-large or if etiology unclear
CT chest with contrast if complex/loculated
Lab analysis: LDH, protein, cell count (Light's criteria)

Emphysema.definition: Destruction of alveolar walls leading to hyperinflation, seen as increased lucency and flattened diaphragms.

severity_grades.severe: Barrel chest, bullae visible, severely flattened diaphragms

follow_up: Pulmonary function tests (spirometry with bronchodilator)
High-resolution CT for phenotyping
Smoking cessation counseling
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level if young/non-smoker

interactions.Pneumothorax: Emphysema is a risk factor for spontaneous pneumothorax — examine carefully

Fibrosis.definition: Pulmonary fibrosis — reticular opacities, volume loss, and architectural distortion indicating chronic scarring.

severity_grades.moderate: Obvious reticular opacities with traction bronchiectasis

follow_up: High-resolution CT chest for pattern classification (UIP, NSIP, etc.)
Pulmonary function tests (FVC, DLCO)
Rheumatology workup (ANA, RF) to exclude connective tissue disease
Pulmonology referral for management

Hernia.definition: Hiatal hernia — herniation of abdominal contents through the diaphragmatic hiatus, seen as retrocardiac soft tissue density.

severity_grades.moderate: Large hiatal hernia with visible gastric bubble above diaphragm
severity_grades.severe: Paraesophageal hernia with organoaxial volvulus risk

follow_up: Barium swallow or upper GI series for characterization
Upper endoscopy if symptomatic (GERD, dysphagia)
Surgical referral for paraesophageal hernias
CT abdomen if concern for volvulus or incarceration

Infiltration.definition: Non-specific term for ill-defined opacities that may represent infection, inflammation, edema, or hemorrhage.
follow_up: Clinical correlation is essential — fever, cough, dyspnea?
If infectious: blood cultures, sputum, empiric antibiotics
CT chest if non-resolving or diagnostic uncertainty
Follow-up CXR in 4-6 weeks

interactions.Consolidation: Dense infiltrate with air bronchograms may represent consolidation/pneumonia
interactions.Edema: Bilateral perihilar infiltrates may represent pulmonary edema rather than infection

Mass.definition: Pulmonary mass — a rounded opacity > 3 cm. Highly suspicious for malignancy until proven otherwise.

severity_grades.mild: Solitary nodule 1-3 cm (technically a nodule, not mass)
severity_grades.severe: Mass with mediastinal invasion, lymphadenopathy, or satellite nodules

follow_up: CT chest with contrast — URGENT
PET-CT for staging if mass confirmed
Tissue biopsy (CT-guided or bronchoscopic)
Oncology and thoracic surgery referral
Comparison with prior imaging if available

interactions.Atelectasis: Post-obstructive atelectasis distal to mass — suggests endobronchial component
interactions.Effusion: Mass + effusion raises concern for malignant pleural effusion — stage IV

Nodule.definition: Pulmonary nodule — a rounded opacity <= 3 cm. May be benign or malignant; follow-up depends on size and risk factors.

severity_grades.mild: < 6 mm — likely benign, follow Fleischner criteria

follow_up: Follow Fleischner Society guidelines based on size and risk
CT chest for characterization (margins, calcification, enhancement)
Compare with prior imaging to assess growth
PET-CT if > 8 mm and intermediate/high risk

Pleural_Thickening.definition: Thickening of the pleural lining, seen as a band of soft tissue density along the chest wall.

severity_grades.severe: Circumferential thickening (trapped lung) or calcified plaques

follow_up: CT chest for detailed assessment
Occupational history (asbestos exposure)
PFTs if restrictive symptoms
Biopsy if nodular thickening (rule out mesothelioma)

interactions.Effusion: Thickening + persistent effusion — rule out malignant mesothelioma

Pneumonia.definition: Infection of the lung parenchyma, seen as focal or diffuse opacities with clinical signs of infection.

severity_grades.mild: Focal opacity, single lobe, hemodynamically stable
severity_grades.severe: Bilateral diffuse opacities, sepsis, respiratory failure

follow_up: CURB-65 or PSI score for severity assessment
Blood cultures x2, sputum culture, procalcitonin
Empiric antibiotics per local guidelines
Supplemental oxygen if SpO2 < 94%
Follow-up CXR at 6-8 weeks to confirm resolution
CT if non-resolving to exclude underlying malignancy

interactions.Effusion: Parapneumonic effusion — thoracentesis if large; rule out empyema
interactions.Consolidation: Consolidation is the typical radiographic appearance of pneumonia

Pneumothorax.definition: Air in the pleural space causing lung collapse. Seen as a visceral pleural line with absent lung markings peripherally.

severity_grades.mild: Small apical pneumothorax < 2 cm at apex
severity_grades.severe: Tension pneumothorax — mediastinal shift, hemodynamic compromise

follow_up: Small + stable: observation, serial CXR q6h, high-flow O2
Moderate: aspiration or small-bore chest drain
Tension: IMMEDIATE needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL) then chest tube
CT if recurrent or underlying lung disease suspected

interactions.Emphysema: Secondary pneumothorax in emphysema — higher recurrence risk, consider pleurodesis
